coredata.dc:title: Porous silicon-polyvinylidene fluoride-carbon dots based portable temperature sensor
coredata.dc:creator: Hernández-Rivera, Daniel
Hernandez-Ramires, Pablo
Suaste-Gómez, Ernesto
Agarwal, Vivechana
coredata.dc:description: Optical sensors are an attractive alternative to semiconductor-based sensors for temperature measurement in harsh environments. Based on Porous Silicon (PSi) Fabry-Perot interferometer, this work presents a novel way of sensing temperature with PSi hybrids using a polymeric nanocomposite of polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) and carbon dots (CDs). The operation is based on the temperature-induced changes in the refractive index of PVDF, which in turn changes the effective refractive index of the PSi hybrid. The dual temperature sensing was based on the simultaneous monitoring of the peak wavelength redshift of the Fabry-Perot interference fringes, and the measurement of the intensity of the reflectance spectrum at a specific wavelength. Both measurements showed excellent linear responses, with coefficients of determination greater than 0.99, revealing the highest sensitivity of 0.2 nm/°C for the sample with the highest content of CDs. Apart from the enhanced temperature dependence of the dielectric constant of PVDF, which improved the sensitivity of the proposed PSi/PVDF-CDs based sensors, the hybrid structure facilitates the formation of β phase up to 91.8%. The proposed silicon-based temperature sensor being very large-scale integration (VLSI) compatible, open the way towards the possible formation of free-standing PSi/PVDF-CDs films with a possible application as biocompatible micro temperature sensors.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs and porous silicon
Preparation and characterization of hybrid PSi/PVDF-CDs structures

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Absorbance spectra of CDs and its composite PVDF-CDs. In both samples two typical absorption bands (275 and 332 nm) of CDs prepared in DMF can be observed. (b) Comparison of the photoluminescence of CDs, pristine PVDF, and PVDF-CD composite film (excitation wavelength 270 nm). An increase in photoluminescence intensity at certain wavelengths in the visible spectrum is evident when CDs are added to the PVDF polymer matrix.

(a) Temperature dependence of the dielectric constant at 1 kHz of (a) pure PVDF and PVDF containing 5 ml CDs/DMFI. Inset: Loss tangent behavior of PVDF samples with and without CDs. (b) Maximum dielectric constant and loss tangent of PVDF nanocomposites containing 0 ml, 1 ml, 2 ml, and 5 ml of CDs/DMFI, measured at 100 °C. The dielectric constant increase as the quantity of CDs increases in the PVDF matrix.

(a) Cross-sectional view under scanning electron microscope showing the 3 μm thickness of the PSi film (Inset: corresponding reflectance spectrum and top view under daylight) and (b) Top-view of the porous silicon surface (Inset: Pore size distribution of the sample with 73% porosity).

(a) Reflectance spectra of a PSi sample before and after infiltrating the polymer (Inset: natural light photograph of a PSi sample with infiltrated PVDF-CD composite). (b) The reflectance spectrum of a free-standing PSi/PVDF-CDs sample showing Fabry-Perot interference fringes. Inset: The PSi/PVDF film as seen under daylight. (c) SEM micrograph of back part of the free-standing PSi/PVDF-CD films. (d) EDS spectra of the back part of the free-standing PSi/PVDF film where we can observe the presence of Si and F which are indicative elements of PSi and PVDF.

(a) FTIR spectra of the PVDF and PVDF-CDs layers formed on the PSi samples. In these spectra, it is possible to observe an increase in the characteristic bands of the β-phase (1275 and 840 cm−1), and a decrease in the characteristic band of the α-phase (763 cm−1) of PVDF after the incorporation of CDs. (b) Photoluminescence spectra of the PSi/PVDF and PSi/PVDF-CDs hybrid structure reveals the increased emission from 400 to 550 nm due to the presence of CDs. Additionally, an emission around 600 nm is observed for both samples, is indicative of an emission generated by the PSi structure.

Shift of the reflectance spectra as a function of temperature for each sample: (a) PSi/PVDF, (b) PSi/PVDF-CDs1, (c) PSi/PVDF-CDs2, and (d) PSi/PVDF-CDs20, with respect to the temperature change. The sample with a higher content of CDs have more sensitivity; a higher CDs content, a greater shift of reflectance curves. The behavior of the hybrid samples is completely different to the behavior of the PSi control samples without polymer shown in Fig. S2.

Measured optical response of the proposed sensor as a function of the temperature, in terms of wavelength shift and intensity variation at specific wavelengths in the reflectance spectrum: (a) PSi/PVDF, (b) PSi/PVDF-CDs1, (c) PSi/PVDF-CDs2, and (d) PSi/PVDF-CDs20. Fabry Perot fringe shift measurement reveals that the samples with a higher CD content results in a higher shift in the spectrum (i.e., signature of more sensitivity). Optical reflectance intensity measurement reveals that the intensity of the optical signal decreases with respect to the temperature.

(a) The shift in the effective optical thickness (2nL; n being the refractive index and L the physical thickness), when heated from 250 C to 1000C vs amounts of CDs incorporated in the hybrid structure. The higher CD content reveals higher shift that corresponds to enhanced sensitivity of the sensor Inset: EOT shift for the sample with better sensitivity to temperature. (b) Amplitud of FFT peak vs Temprature for PSi/PVDF-CDs20 (the inversely proportional linear behavior of the amplitude of one of the FFT peaks with respect to temperature is revealed).
Sensitivity and the coefficient of determination of PSi/PVDF hybrid structures as a function of CD concentration.
Comparison of the main characteristics of different optical temperature sensors with similar operation range.

head.ce:title: Porous silicon-polyvinylidene fluoride-carbon dots based portable temperature sensor

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Optical sensors are an attractive alternative to semiconductor-based sensors for temperature measurement in harsh environments. Based on Porous Silicon (PSi) Fabry-Perot interferometer, this work presents a novel way of sensing temperature with PSi hybrids using a polymeric nanocomposite of polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) and carbon dots (CDs). The operation is based on the temperature-induced changes in the refractive index of PVDF, which in turn changes the effective refractive index of the PSi hybrid. The dual temperature sensing was based on the simultaneous monitoring of the peak wavelength redshift of the Fabry-Perot interference fringes, and the measurement of the intensity of the reflectance spectrum at a specific wavelength. Both measurements showed excellent linear responses, with coefficients of determination greater than 0.99, revealing the highest sensitivity of 0.2 nm/°C for the sample with the highest content of CDs. Apart from the enhanced temperature dependence of the dielectric constant of PVDF, which improved the sensitivity of the proposed PSi/PVDF-CDs based sensors, the hybrid structure facilitates the formation of β phase up to 91.8%. The proposed silicon-based temperature sensor being very large-scale integration (VLSI) compatible, open the way towards the possible formation of free-standing PSi/PVDF-CDs films with a possible application as biocompatible micro temperature sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Optical sensors are an effective alternative for measurements under harsh environmental conditions [1–3]. Electromagnetic interferences, corrosive atmospheres, and excessive humidity are elements that can significantly affect measurements made with conventional electronic sensors due to some related limitations of the semiconductor materials and the accompanying electronic circuits. Additionally, some of the materials employed in such conventional sensors can be toxic to humans and environmentally unfriendly [4,5]. Hence, it is important to implement the sensors made of biocompatible and eco-friendly materials [6,7] that can be used in different environmental conditions.

On the other hand, among the parameters of interest to be measured in the technology area, the temperature continues to be an essential part [8]. Technological progress constantly creates new paradigms that make the use conventional temperature sensors difficult. High humidity environments, gassy and corrosive atmospheres, or explosive solvents can make the measurement of temperature, with the conventional sensors, difficult. Most of optical temperature sensors can allow remote sensing, therefore the sensing system does not need to be exposed to harsh environments [1,8]. Highly sensitive optical temperature sensors based on surface plasmonic resonance (SPR) effect have been reported recently [9–12]. Temperature sensors based on interferometry have also demonstrated great versatility due to their high sensitivity, accuracy, and resistance to electromagnetic interference. In general, the Michelson [13], Mach-Zehnder [14,15], Fizeau [16], Fabry-Perot [15,17–19], interferometry techniques are used to design temperature sensors. Most of these sensors are implemented with optical fibers, so there must be a direct connection between the sensitive optical fiber and the optical measurement system. The phenomenon of optical destructive interference has been used in other complex structures. Photonic crystals (PhCs) are dielectric periodic structures with different refractive indices, which can control the propagation of certain wavelength range in the medium. Such optical structures have been used for sensing temperature in combination with fiber optic sensors [20,21], ring resonator based sensors [22,23], polymer-PhCs sensors [24], Tamm resonance based sensors [25–27], and Fano resonance based sensors [28,29].

Porous silicon can be used to create photonic crystals and other interferometric structures [25,26,30–33]. The basic structure of PSi (i.e. monolayer) generates interference fringes due to the presence of two refractive index changes, one between the air and the porous silicon structure, and the other one between the porous structure and the Si base [34]. The porous structure can be filled with some material to modify the effective refractive index of the medium [30,35,36]. A change in the refractive index of the porous structure causes a change in the interference fringes of the observed reflectance spectrum. This phenomenon has been exploited to design sensors for biomolecules [37], gases [38,39], solvents [40], etc [30].

Polymer-PSi hybrid structures are based on filling the pores of PSi with a polymer [30] and have demonstrated multiple applications in optical sensing [41], photoluminescent structures [42], cell culture, etc. One material that stands out among the polymers is PVDF. The most interesting properties of this polymer are its resistance to radiation and chemical attack, biocompatibility, along with ferroelectric and dielectric properties [43–45]. Apart from the superior physical and chemical properties with respect to the other polymers, the thermal stability of PVDF can be exploited for temperature measurement applications in harsh environments as evidenced by several groups e.g., under X-rays/UV radiation [43,46], gaseous [47], and corrosive environments [48].

Different nanostructures have been used as fillers to improve the electrical [49], hydrophobic [50], piezoelectric [51–53], and UV absorbance of PVDF [43]. The carbon dots are a kind of photoluminescent and biocompatible nanostructure [54–58] that recently has been used as filler in PVDF polymer matrix [43,49,52,59] and have been shown to improve the dielectric properties of pristine PVDF [49]. The dielectric constant in PVDF is higher than in other polymers due to the ordering of its electric dipoles [51,53,60]. The dielectric constant is sensitive to temperature since dipole rearrangement is facilitated with increasing temperature [61] (i.e., higher the temperature, higher the dielectric constant).

As the dielectric constant and refractive index are directly linked with each other, a change in the temperature of a material such as PVDF would cause a change in its refractive index (as its dielectric constant changes). In the present work, a nanostructured porous silicon film based Fabry-Perot(FB) interferometer, filled with a PVDF/CDs nanocomposite, was proposed as an optical temperature sensor. The mechanism of operation of this temperature sensor is based on the changes in the FB interference fringes of the PSi/PVDF hybrid structure (as a function of temperature), caused by the corresponding change of the dielectric constant of the polymer nanocomposite inside the porous PSi structure.

PVDF powder and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, Anhydride, purity: 99.8%) were used to fabricate films and the polymer solution infiltrated into the PSi samples. Aqueous hydrofluoric acid (HF, concentration: 48%.), ethanol (purity: 99.9%), and glycerol (purity: 99.8%) were used to make the electrolyte mixture needed to fabricate PSi. In the case of CDs, leaves of Pithecellobium dulce (Guamuchil), collected in Cuernavaca, Morelos, Mexico, were used as precursor material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs and porous silicon
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots were obtained by carbonization technique using Guamuchil leaves as precursor. The detailed procedure is given in Ref. [62]. In brief, first, the leaves were washed, dried, crushed and heated at 400 °C for 2 h in a muffle furnace. The carbonized material was mechanically ground to a fine powder and dispersed in DMF (10 mg of powder per 1 ml of DMF) for 4 h of ultrasonic bath. The suspension was centrifuged at 13,000 rpm and the filtered supernatant (CDs in DMF) was used to incorporate the synthesized CDs into the PVDF polymeric matrix. The quantum yield (QY) of the CDs was calculated as 24% using a Rhodamine 6G in water (QYx = 92%) as a reference [63].

The porous silicon samples were fabricated by electrochemical anodization of boron-doped p++-type silicon wafers (resistivity: 1–5 mΩ) with (100) orientation. The process was performed by supplying a current of 52 mA/cm2 for 192 s using an electrolyte consisting of a mixture of aqueous HF, ethanol, and glycerol in a 3:7:1 ratio. The PSi samples were used without any additional treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of hybrid PSi/PVDF-CDs structures
ce:section.ce:para: A polymeric solution was prepared by dissolving PVDF powder in a CDs/DMF solution at 65 °C (2 h) with continuous stirring. The resulting homogeneous solution (PVDF/DMF-CDs) was deposited, by drop-casting, on a glass substrate and dried at 65° for 2 h. Finally, to remove DMF residues, the samples were stored for 72 h at room temperature before use. The resulting films were used for further characterization.

All PVDF-CDs’ solutions were prepared at 65 °C using constant stirring. Different concentrations of CDs were prepared by diluting them with pure DMF (0 μl (control), 50 μl, 100 μl, and 1000 μl of the CDs (in DMF) stock solution) to obtain PVDF-CDs polymeric solutions (in DMF) with concentrations of 0, 1, 2, and 20 ml of CDs (in DMF) stock soln per gram of PVDF. For the preparation of the hybrid structure, 30 μl of the PVDF-CDs polymer solutions were deposited by drop-casting onto the PSi samples at 65 °C. These samples were kept at this temperature for 2 h to remove excess DMF. Finally, hybrid samples were obtained and named as PSi/PVDF (control), PSi/PVDF-CDs1, PSi/PVDF-CDs2, and PSi/PVDF-CDs20, according to their CD content (0, 1, 2, and 20 ml CDs stock suspension in DMF).

Additionally, the PVDF-filled porous silicon layer was mechanically removed from the Si layer to obtain free-standing samples. The free-standing PSi films supported by PVDF (PSi/PVDF-CDs films) was also obtained and used for further characterization.

PVDF films with and without CDs were studied by UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy to know if the CDs were incorporated into the PVDF polymer matrix without losing their optical properties. The absorbance and photoluminescence spectra were acquired with a beam PerkinElmer Lambda 950 Spectrophotometer and a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer, respectively.

The sensing phenomenon used in this work is based on the change of the effective refractive index of PSi/PVDF samples associated with the change of the dielectric constant of the polymer caused by a temperature variation. Based on different investigations, the incorporation of CDs into the PVDF matrix is expected to improve the sensitivity of its dielectric constant to temperature. The capacitance of the films with different quantities of CDs was measured at temperatures from 25 to 100 °C using an LCR bridge (HM818- Rohde & Schwarz) to know the effect of temperature and the CDs on the dielectric constant of PVDF.

The monolayer porous silicon samples were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (Field emission SEM). Diameter J software was used to analyze the micrographs and obtain the average pore size of the samples and the thickness. Additionally, the reflectance spectrum of the PSi samples (filled with air, ethanol) was obtained through an Ocean Optics reflectance spectrometer. This information was used to calculate the porosity percentage of the samples using Bruggeman approximation.

Reflectance spectra of the PSi samples before and after PVDF deposition were obtained to analyze the change in interference fringes associated with a change in refractive index caused by polymer infiltration into the pores of the PSi samples. Changes in PVDF crystallinity caused by the CDs were studied using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra-Varian 660-IR FTIR spectrophotometer.

SEM and EDX characterizations were performed on the inner part (the part of the PSi layer that was attached to the crystalline Si) of the free-standing PSi/PVDF film to confirm the infiltration of PVDF into the porous structure.

In order to test the response of the hybrid structure as a function of temperature, PSi/PVDF and PSi/PVDF samples with different amounts of CDs were subjected to temperature changes from 25 to 100 °C while simultaneously measuring the reflectance spectra. The limiting factor for choosing this temperature range is the melting point of the PVDF, i.e., the temperatures >100 °C, could modify its crystalline structure and hence the properties of the polymer matrix. The shift of the interference patterns was plotted with respect to temperature to analyze and compare the sensitivity of the PVDF and PVDF samples with CDs. Additionally, the reflectance spectra of pure PSi samples were measured under temperature changes before polymer incorporation(control). This analysis was performed to verify the temperature dependence of the refractive index of pristine PSi samples. A J-type thermometer was used to measure the temperature changes in the samples.

Fig. 1 a shows the absorbance spectra of the CDs (dispersed in DMF) and the PVDF/CDs films. In both spectra, we can observe bands located around 275 nm and 332 nm, which are attributed to π-π* and n-π* transitions, respectively. These absorption bands are similar to those observed/reported for other CDs fabricated in DMF [64]. Concerning the photoluminescence of the synthetized CDs, the estimated QY was 24%. In Fig. 1b we can observe the photoluminescence spectra of PVDF films with and without CDs. With respect to the control (pristine PVDF), the occurrence of an additional band in the visible region (around 475 nm) observed in the PVDF/CDs composite films, confirms the incorporation of CDs into the PVDF matrix without significant loss in their photoluminescent properties.

The temperature dependence of the dielectric constant (εr) of PVDF films doped with CDs (Fig. 2 ). reveals an enhanced sensitivity of the εr to temperature with an increase in the CD content. The improvement of the εr of PVDF by the addition of CDs has been reported in literature, in which it has been established that the incorporation of CDs in the PVDF matrix produces an improvement in polarization (enhancement in the dielectric constant) [49]. The increase of the dielectric constant with increasing temperature in PVDF occurs for two reasons: (a) at higher temperatures, the electric dipoles can be reoriented more easily [65], which results in a better polarization capability, i.e., there is a higher dielectric constant; (b)the second phenomenon is related to the semi-crystalline structure of PVDF, in which an interfacial structure is formed between the crystalline phase and the amorphous phase [66]. On the other hand, the improvement of the sensitivity of the εr to temperature for samples containing CDs could be attributed to the interfacial polarization caused by the fillers [66,67]. With the addition of CDs to the PVDF structure, two other types of interfacial structures occur: the first interfacial structure originates from between the fillers and the PVDF polymeric matrix, and the second one due to the formation of the internal barrier-layer capacitor (IBLC), it is between the fillers and the enlargement of the free volume [66]. All interfacial structures contribute the dielectric relaxation processes. One aspect to note is that dielectric losses also increase as the number of CDs in PVDF increases, which is attributed to an increase in electrical conductivity caused by the CDs. This fact should be considered very important because although the addition of CDs improves the dielectric properties of PVDF, it can also increase the dielectric losses.

Fig. 3 a shows a micrograph of the monolayer samples (3 μm thickness) used in the present work. A daylight photograph of the PSi samples can be seen in the upper right of this figure. The typical reflectance spectrum of PS films with Fabry-Perot interference fringes caused by reflections at the Si–PSi and the PSi-Air interfaces can be observed. The pore area distribution revealed from the top view of the porous surface (Fig. 3b and the corresponding inset) was determined through ImageJ software. The average pore area was approx. 316 nm2, and the porosity, based on Bruggeman approximation calculations, was about 73%. This final porosity of the sample is very similar to the initial anticipated value of porosity (based on the calibration curves) taken during the sample preparation.

Fig. 4 a shows the reflectance spectra of a PSi sample before and after the PVDF was infiltrated. The spectra show a shift of the interference fringes, indicating the infiltration of the polymer into the PSi structure. Inset: it can be seen a PSi sample with PVDF infiltrated under natural light. The reflectance spectrum (Fig. 4b) of a free-standing PSi/PVDF-CDs sample shows an interference pattern similar to that of the PSi/PVDF samples. The coloration of the PSi/PVDF film under daylight is evidence of the porous structure supported by the polymer. The color of the free-standing PSi/PVDF-CDs film could be induced by the double-sided tape on which the film was placed. Fig. 4c exhibits the back part of the free-standing porous structure (lifted-off from the Si wafer) supported within the PVDF matrix. During the EDS analysis (Fig. 4d) performed on the innermost layer of the PVDF-PSi hybrid structure, the presence of the signal corresponding to Si and F (indicative of the presence of PVDF) verified the correct infiltration of PVDF into the PSi structure.

PVDF can have different crystalline phases, i.e., the α, β, or γ phase predominance will be indicative of the physical properties of PVDF. In this work, we intend to use PVDF with a predominance of β phase since it is the most polar structure (responsible for its ferroelectric properties) and improves the dielectric properties of the polymer [68,69]. Fig. 5 a shows the FTIR spectra of pristine PVDF and PVDF with CDs (film layers formed on the PSi samples). It is possible to observe that the β-phase peaks at 840 cm−1 and 1275 cm−1 increased, and the peak corresponding to the α-phase (763 cm−1) decreased after the incorporation of CDs. Using equation (1) [70], the β phase fraction (F(β)) for samples with and without CDs was obtained.

In equation (1) Aβ and Aα are the absorbances at 840 cm−1 and 763 cm−1, respectively. F(β) for PVDF and PVDF-CDs were 74% and 91.8%, then the β-phase of the polymer improved with CDs. Another interesting fact is that the F(β) for PVDF samples deposited on glass (see FTIR spectra in Fig. S1-Supplementary Information) was 29.8%, which indicates that the porous structure of PSi facilitates the β phase in PVDF when infiltrated. This fact could be used to generate PVDF sensors with an improved piezoelectric effect. This fact is in accordance with the previous studies reported in the literature where the increase in β-phase by incorporating CDs into the crystal structure of PVDF [52,59] was observed. On the other hand, the fluorescent properties of CDs are also observed in PVDF/CDs nanocomposite on the PSi structure in the form of a wide band (Fig. 5b) which can be deconvoluted. The emission wavelengths of the polymer nanocomposite between 400 and 550 nm are attributed to the presence of CDs, as previously demonstrated in the paper published by our group [62]. The emission peak around 600 nm is attributed to the PSi. As the photoluminescent properties of PSi samples only appear after the infiltration of the polymer, the fluorescence could be generated by the microfractures in the pore walls caused by the incorporation of the polymer dissolved in volatile solvent DMF. These cracks could help to enhance the quantum confinement effect causing photoluminescence in the originally non-luminescent mesoporous Si [71].

Firstly, Fig. S2 shows the tests performed to evaluate the refractive index behavior of pristine porous silicon (as control sample) as a function of temperature. Due to the use of highly doped p++ for the formation of porous silicon, no shift is observed in the interference fringes of the reflectance spectrum was observed in pristine PSi film as the temperature changed from 25° to 100 °C. Considering this fact, the hybrid PSi/PVDF-CDs samples were tested as optical temperature sensors. The principle of operation is based on the change of the dielectric constant of the PVDF infiltrated into the porous structure of the PSi sample caused by temperature variation. This change in the dielectric constant also means a variation in the refractive index of the effective medium of the PSi/PVDF, which causes a shift of the interference fringes of the reflectance spectrum. Fig. 6 shows the temperature-induced shift of the interference curves of PSi/PVDF samples with different amounts of CDs. Fig. 7 exhibits a wavelength shift and intensity variation of the reflectance spectrum of the samples for temperature changes from 25° to 100 °C. All samples with CDs show a linear trend with a coefficient of determination R2 greater than 0.99 (Table 1 ), which confirms their excellent linear response. The PSi/PVDF sample without CDs shows an R2 of around 0.98. The sensitivity of the samples ranges from 0.13 nm/°C for the sample without CDs to 0.20 nm/°C for the sample with the highest content of CDs. This sensitivity is interesting considering the operating range (25–100 °C) and the linear response of the proposed sensors.

In Table S1, we can observe the linear equations for both the heating and cooling tests. Samples with concentrations of 2 and 20 ml of CDs/DMF from the stock solution (with 7.8 and 10.8% as relative difference in sensitivity, respectively) showed a greater difference in sensitivity between the heating and cooling stages. This can also be seen in Fig. 7c and d, in which the wavelength of the studied interference fringe does not return to the same point of origin. This fact could be caused by the increase in dielectric losses for the samples with a higher number of CDs. However, the PVDF nanocomposites with nanofillers are more sensitive to temperature [43–45].

On the other hand, Fig. 6 also shows the intensity response of the reflectance spectrum measured at specific wavelengths. Like the wavelength shift response, the intensity response shows a linear trend. In this case, the sensor response is inversely proportional to temperature. Table S2 (ref to SI) shows linear equations and linearity of the light intensity measurements of the temperature sensing. The intensity response of the samples with CDs shows an excellent linear response (R2 > 0.99). The sensitivity is higher for the sample with higher number of CDs (PSi/PVDF-CDs20), but there is no clear tendency towards the improvement of the sensitivity with respect to the content of CDs. This fact can be attributed to the deformation of the reflectance curves observed in Fig. 6, which in turn can result in certain errors in the intensity measurements. The intensity measurements in the reflectance spectrum are more sensitive to measurement errors caused by motion or environmental noise. Despite some issues, it was possible to perform dual optical signals as a function of temprature (reflected light intensity and shift of the Fabry-Perot interference fringes), which corresponds to a more exact response.

Finally, in Fig. 8 we can see the temperature dependence of the optical thickness of the PSi/PVDF samples with different amounts of CDs. This parameter was obtained by using FFT of the reflectance spectra (RIFTS method [72]). As expected, the enhanced change in the effective optical thickness (EOT) for the samples with more CDs, confirms an enhancement in the change of the effective refractive index of the corresponding samples. This fact verifies the initial hypothesis in which it was established that a change in the dielectric constant of the PVDF immersed in the pores of the PSi would produce a variation in the effective refractive index of the hybrid structure formed by the PSi and the PVDF. The sample with 20 ml of CDs/DMF showed a change of about 450 nm (EOT) which is within the sensitivity range of other PSi-based optical sensors, indicative of its potential for its use as an optical temperature sensor.

Table 2 shows the characteristics of other optical temperature sensors. Comparing the sensitivity, linearity, operating range, and properties of other sensors, the data obtained in the present work are indicative of the potential of the proposed sensor for measurements in the range of 25–100 °C. Additionally, for technological applications, the tested temperature range of the sensor is useful for applications in food industry [73], power systems [74–76], clinical area [77–80], and robotics [81], where the operational range of the required sensor is lower than 100 °C (similar to the proposed sensor).

Apart from being VLSI compatible, the obtained sensitivity of the proposed sensor can be useful for multiple applications as evidenced in different scientific papers on optical temperature sensors [84,86–89]. Although the sensitivity may not be the main differentiator from other temperature sensors, the optical functioning, the portability, VLSI compatibility, the protection offered by the PVDF, and the low cost of fabrication of the nanostructures (CDs) are novel elements that are advantageous with respect to the other sensors. In addition, the free-standing structure (Fig. 4b) could be easily adapted to different surfaces [90,91] to measure temperature. The free-standing films could be adapted to an optical fiber [92,93] and other optoelectronic devices to generate new types of remote optical sensors.

The present work shows the fabrication and performance of a temperature sensor based on a biocompatible PSi -PVDF/CDs based hybrid structure. It was possible to create a fluorescent polymer nanocomposite (PVDF/CDs) with a broad emission in the visible spectrum. The addition of CDs revealed an increase in the dielectric constant of PVDF by 34%. The infiltration of the nanocomposite into the porous structure enhanced the fraction of β-crystalline phase of the PVDF up to 91.8%. The incorporation of PVDF/CDs composite contributed to the temperature dependence of the refractive index of the hybrid structure (PSi-PVDF/CDs), which was used to create a dual temperature sensor with excellent linearity (R2 > 0.99) and a sensitivity of 201 p.m./°C in the range of 25–100 °C. Although, the sensitivity of the εr to temperature in PVDF can also be modified by combining it with other types of nanoparticles of complex fabrication, our work demonstrates the usage of CDs, synthesized with an extremely simple and sustainable/green process, as fillers.

The proposed sensor can be useful for temperature measurement in highly humid, corrosive, gaseous, radiative and highly noisy environments. The free-standing PSi/PVDF-CDs films could be used to create a compact micrometric temperature sensor. The demonstration of the PSi-PVDF/CDs hybrid structure performance as a temperature sensor using a PSi nanostructured film, opens the possibility of enhanced sensitivity with the usage of PSi based photonic structures to improve the optical response of the proposed optical temperature sensor.

Daniel Hernández-Rivera: Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft; Pablo Hernandez-Ramires: Porous silicon sample fabrication; Ernesto Suaste-Gómez: Resources Vivechana Agarwal: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition.
